scenario: CO opens, BB calls. Flop K♠7♥2♣. Hero (CO) holds A♣K♦. BB checks.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Top pair top kicker (A♣K♦ on K-7-2) is STRONG VALUE. You're the preflop raiser and in position (CO vs BB) — bet for value across multiple streets; value betting is where most micro profit comes from. You want worse Kx, pairs, and draws to pay you off.

scenario: MP opens, BTN calls. Flop J♦8♠5♥, MP bets, BTN calls. Turn 2♣. Hero (MP) holds J♠9♣. MP is first to act; BTN has been tight.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Top pair with a WEAK kicker (J9 on J-8-5) is MEDIUM STRENGTH — showdown value, but dominated by better Jx. You're OOP (MP vs BTN) as the aggressor, and the tight caller's range is firming up. Pot control: check, or thin-value bet only vs weaker players. Don't bloat the pot with a one-pair weak-kicker hand. (If the kicker were an A/K, this would be top pair TOP kicker = Strong — the kicker splits the tiers.)

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop T♠9♦4♣. Hero (BTN) holds Q♠J♠. BB checks.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: This is a DRAW — and a strong one: an open-ended straight draw (any K or 8, 8 outs) + two overcards + a backdoor flush. You're in position (BTN vs BB) as the aggressor. Default: semi-bluff — you have equity if called AND fold equity now, and can barrel many turns. The draw upgrades you from 'nothing yet' to a betting hand; don't check it back.

scenario: CO opens, BB calls. BB is a loose-passive calling station. Flop K♣9♠4♦, CO bets, BB calls. Turn 7♦. Hero (CO) holds A♥5♥. BB checks.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Ace-high, no pair, no draw is WEAK. You're IP as the aggressor, but the opponent is a calling station who doesn't fold — so a bluff has no fold equity and you have no showdown value worth betting. Check and give up unless you improve. The PLAYER TYPE drives the line: vs a high-fold reg one stab is fine, but never fire at a station with air.

scenario: A TAG 3-bets preflop, Hero calls with K♠T♠ (Hero is out of position). Flop 6♦Q♣J♠. The 3-bettor bets.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Draw — 8 outs (any A or 9) + an overcard, enough equity to continue
explanation: This is a DRAW: any A (4) or any 9 (4) completes a straight = 8 outs (~31% by the river), plus the K overcard adds a few more. Not a weak gutshot — a real open-ended draw. You're OOP as the caller vs a 3-bettor's strong range, so respect it, but 8 outs + overcard is a continue (call, or semi-bluff-raise depending on price/fold equity), not a fold. (Postflop spot only — the preflop call is taken as given.)

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop K♦Q♠5♣, BB checks, BTN bets, BB calls. Turn 3♠, BB checks, BTN bets, BB calls. River 2♥, BB leads big. Hero (BTN) holds K♣J♠.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Hand strength is DYNAMIC — it shifts with the action. K♣J♠ (top pair, J kicker) was decent on the flop, but a passive player who check-called twice then suddenly leads big on a blank river has a range that beats one pair: KQ (two pair), sets, slowplayed monsters. Your top-pair-decent-kicker demotes to a bluff-catcher, and passive players rarely turn a hand into a bluff. Lean fold to the big lead. The action redefines the tier.

scenario: MP opens, BTN calls. Flop 8♥4♣2♦. Hero (MP) holds 8♠8♦. BTN checks.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Strong value (monster) — build the pot; occasionally slowplay one street on a very dry board
explanation: A set is a STRONG VALUE monster. You're IP as the aggressor on a dry board (8-4-2 rainbow, no draws). Default: bet and build toward stacks. You can occasionally slowplay ONE street here since nothing realistically outdraws you cheaply — but the default is to build the pot, not check it down.

scenario: Villain 4-bets preflop, Hero calls with A♣Q♣ (Hero is the caller; Villain's range is QQ+/AK). Flop K-8-4 checks through. Turn blank, Villain bets, Hero calls. River pairs the board, Villain bets.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Weak vs this range — A-high beats nothing the 4-bettor continues with
explanation: A-Q on K-8-4 is ace-high, and vs a tight 4-bet range (QQ+/AK) it's WEAK — it beats nothing villain bets (KK, AA, AK, QQ all beat or dominate it). Facing the river bet with ace-high, you're a bluff-catcher that catches no bluffs (a tight 4-bettor isn't bluffing here), so it's a fold. (Postflop categorization only: your hand is weak vs this range and should give up — the earlier streets are taken as given.)

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop A♦K♠5♥, BB checks. Hero (BTN) holds A♣K♦.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Top two pair (aces and kings) on A♦K♠5♥ is STRONG VALUE. Hero is IP as the aggressor (BTN vs BB). Only a set beats you here — 55, or the unlikely AA/KK after BB just flat-called; every Ax, Kx, and lower two pair is crushed. Note the heart flush draw on the board: bet BIG for value AND protection — you want to charge the flush draws and worse made hands a premium, and there's no reason to keep the pot small when almost nothing beats you. Size up and build toward stacks.

scenario: CO opens, BTN calls. Flop Q♥Q♦7♣, BTN checks. Hero (CO) holds A♥Q♠.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Trip queens with an ace kicker on Q-Q-7 is STRONG VALUE. You're OOP as the aggressor (CO vs BTN). Bet for value — almost nothing beats you (only QQ itself or 77 full). Build the pot; trips on a paired board is a near-monster, just be alert if the board pairs higher later.

scenario: UTG opens, CO calls. Flop 9♠9♦4♣. Hero (UTG) holds A♠9♥.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Trip nines with an ace kicker is STRONG VALUE. You're OOP as the aggressor (UTG vs CO). Bet for value — you dominate the Tx/Jx/pair hands that call. On a paired board your trips want to build the pot; only 44 (full house) or 9x with a better kicker beats you, both rare.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop 7♥6♥2♣, BTN bets, BB check-raises. Hero (BB) holds 8♥5♥.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Strong — play it like value (15-out combo draw)
Draw — but just call, don't raise
explanation: 8♥5♥ on 7♥6♥2♣ is a massive combo DRAW that plays like STRONG value: an open-ended straight draw (8-7-6-5 needs a 9 or 4) PLUS the heart flush draw — roughly 15 outs, often a favorite against one pair. You're OOP as the caller and you've check-raised: correct, because with this much equity you want to build the pot and deny equity now. The combo draw upgrades you to the value end of the spectrum — get aggressive.

scenario: CO opens, BB calls. Flop K♣8♦3♠, BB checks. Hero (CO) holds A♦8♣.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Middle pair (the 8) with an ace kicker on K-8-3 is MEDIUM STRENGTH — showdown value, but any Kx beats you and the board has an overcard to your pair. You're IP as the aggressor (CO vs BB). Pot control: one bet at most, then lean toward checking. Don't build a big pot with middle pair.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop A♠T♦6♣, BB checks, BTN bets, BB calls. Turn 3♥, BB checks. Hero (BTN) holds A♣7♦.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Top pair with a weak kicker (A7 on A-T-6) is MEDIUM. You're IP as the aggressor (BTN vs BB). You beat worse aces and pairs but lose to AT/AJ+/two pair/sets. Pot control — you've bet once; now check back the turn rather than bloating the pot. Get to a cheap showdown.

scenario: MP opens, BB calls. Flop J♣9♦4♠, BB checks. Hero (MP) holds T♠T♥.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: TT on J-9-4 is an UNDERPAIR to the jack = MEDIUM strength (a one-pair hand beaten by any Jx). You're IP as the aggressor (MP vs BB). Pot control — you can bet once for protection/thin value, but don't stack off: an underpair to the top card is not a commit hand. Check-back-ish, fold to heavy aggression.

scenario: CO opens, BTN 3-bets, CO calls. Flop K♦7♠2♣. Hero (CO) holds Q♣Q♥.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: QQ on a K-high board is an UNDERPAIR = MEDIUM. You're OOP as the caller vs a 3-bettor (CO called BTN's 3-bet). A 3-bettor's range has plenty of Kx (AK, KK) plus AA. Your QQ beats their bluffs/AQ but loses to the king-heavy value. Pot control / bluff-catch one street; do NOT stack off an underpair on a K-high board vs a 3-bet range.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop 5♣5♦K♠, BB checks, BTN bets, BB calls. Turn 9♥, BB checks. Hero (BTN) holds 7♦7♠.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: 77 on K-5-5 is a small pair below the king = MEDIUM (one pair, beaten by any Kx and the trip-fives hands). You're IP as the aggressor. Pot control — check back the turn; your sevens have showdown value vs missed overcards but can't stand much pressure. Don't barrel a small pair on a king-high paired board.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop A♠K♦7♠, BB checks. Hero (BTN) holds Q♠J♠.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: Q♠J♠ on A♠K♦7♠ is a DRAW: the nut flush draw (spades) plus a gutshot (a T makes Broadway). You're IP as the aggressor (BTN vs BB). Semi-bluff — you have big equity (flush + straight outs) plus fold equity, and the nut-flush-draw means you're never drawing to a second-best flush. Bet, and barrel good turns.

scenario: CO opens, BB calls. Flop 8♦7♦2♣, CO bets, BB calls. Turn K♠, BB checks. Hero (CO) holds A♦6♦.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Draw — semi-bluff with the nut flush draw
explanation: A♦6♦ on a two-diamond board (8♦7♦2♣) is a DRAW — the nut flush draw (~9 outs, ~35% by the river from the flop). You're IP as the aggressor (CO vs BB). Semi-bluff: barrel the turn with fold equity plus the best possible flush draw. The K turn doesn't improve you, but the nut draw is enough equity to keep applying pressure or take a free card if the price is wrong.

scenario: CO opens, BB calls. Flop T♥9♥3♣, BB checks, CO bets, BB calls. Turn 2♦, BB checks, CO bets, BB calls. River 6♣, BB checks, CO bets. BB to act. Hero (BB) holds Q♥J♥.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: By the river the draws all missed: Q♥J♥ on T-9-3-2-6 is now just Q-high = WEAK. You're OOP as the caller and you've called two streets with a draw that bricked. A missed draw with no showdown value is a check-fold (or a bluff only if you have a strong read and fold equity). Don't pay off the river with queen-high. The category changes when the draw misses.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop 9♠8♠2♦, BB checks. Hero (BTN) holds T♠7♠.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: T♠7♠ on 9♠8♠2♦ is a big combo DRAW: an open-ended straight draw (any J or 6 completes T-9-8-7 into a straight) PLUS the spade flush draw. That's ~15 outs of combined equity. You're IP as the aggressor (BTN vs BB). Strong semi-bluff — huge equity if called plus fold equity now. Bet and barrel good turns; this draw plays like value, not a check-back.

scenario: MP opens, BB calls. Flop K♠Q♦7♣, BB checks. Hero (MP) holds 6♥5♥.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: 6♥5♥ on K-Q-7 is a complete whiff — no pair, no draw, no overcards of value = WEAK. You're IP as the aggressor. A single c-bet bluff is defensible vs a foldy opponent (you have initiative), but there's no hand to build a pot with. Default: one stab at most, then give up. Don't barrel air.

scenario: CO opens, BTN calls. Flop A♥A♦9♠, CO bets, BTN calls. Turn 4♣, CO checks, BTN checks. River 2♦, CO checks, BTN bets. Hero (CO) holds K♣Q♣.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Weak — fold to the river bet
explanation: K♣Q♣ on A-A-9-4-2 is king-high, no pair = WEAK. You're OOP as the aggressor but you gave up (checked turn and river). When BTN bets the river after you showed weakness, king-high beats only a bluff — and you have no reason to think this is one. Fold. King-high is not a bluff-catcher worth a river call here.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop J♦8♣3♠, BB checks, BTN bets, BB check-raises. Hero (BTN) holds A♣5♣.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: A♣5♣ on J-8-3 is ace-high with no pair and no draw = WEAK. You're IP as the aggressor, but a check-raise on a dryish board reps real strength (Jx+, sets, two pair). Ace-high has no equity to continue and no showdown value worth defending. Fold to the check-raise. Don't 'float' with nothing.

scenario: UTG opens, BB calls. Flop Q♠J♠4♥, BB checks. Hero (UTG) holds 7♦7♣.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
explanation: 77 on Q-J-4 is an underpair beneath TWO overcards (Q and J), no draw = effectively WEAK here (closer to weak than medium — it beats only a pure bluff or a lower pair, and it's behind every Qx/Jx plus draws). You're OOP as the aggressor. One small c-bet is fine for protection/fold equity, but treat it as give-up-able: fold to a raise or a second barrel. Low pair on a high two-overcard board is not a hand to commit.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. BB is a calling station. Flop K♦9♣4♠, BTN bets, BB calls. Turn 9♦, BB checks. Hero (BTN) holds K♣Q♠.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Strong value — bet all three streets for thin value vs a station
explanation: Top pair good kicker vs a CALLING STATION is effectively STRONG VALUE for value-betting purposes — against a player who never folds and calls with worse Kx/9x/pairs, you should bet all three streets for value, NOT pot-control. The player type upgrades your line: vs a thinking TAG you'd pot-control TPGK, but vs a station you milk it. Same hand, different plan based on the opponent.

scenario: CO opens, BB calls. Flop 7♣6♣5♦, BB checks, CO bets, BB calls. Turn K♠, BB checks, CO bets, BB calls. River K♥, BB leads big. Hero (CO) holds 9♣8♣.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Strong value — but a paired board means re-evaluate vs a big lead
explanation: 9♣8♣ on 7-6-5-K-K made a straight on the flop — STRONG. But hand strength is DYNAMIC: the board paired kings on the river AND a passive player suddenly leads big into you. A straight loses to a full house (any Kx, 77/66/55) now possible. You're IP as the aggressor, but a big river lead from a passive player on a paired board is a yellow flag. Still usually a call (your straight beats most of their range), but recognize you're no longer the stone nuts — this is the 'strong, but re-evaluate on the paired runout' lesson.

scenario: BTN opens, BB calls. Flop T♠T♦6♣, BB checks, BTN bets, BB calls. Turn 2♠, BB checks, BTN bets, BB calls. River 6♠, BB checks. Hero (BTN) holds A♠Q♠.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Weak — give up, no showdown value
explanation: A♠Q♠ on T-T-6-2-6 is ace-high — WEAK, no pair, no draw got there (the spade flush didn't complete). You're IP as the aggressor and you've fired two streets; a third barrel into a double-paired board only folds out hands that already beat ace-high rarely and gets called by any pair. Give up and check behind / fold. No showdown value worth betting; don't triple-barrel air.

scenario: CO opens, BB calls. Flop 4♥4♦K♣, BB checks, CO bets, BB calls. Turn 8♠, BB leads. Hero (CO) holds A♦K♦.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Strong value — but note the paired board
explanation: AK on K-4-4 is top pair top kicker = STRONG VALUE — you beat all Kx and pairs. You're IP as the aggressor. The paired board (44) means trips are possible (any 4), but those are few combos; a BB lead on the turn is more often a king, a worse pair, or a bluff than quad/trip fours. Keep value-betting, but stay aware that on a paired board your one-pair-plus-top-kicker isn't invulnerable to the rare 4x. Default: continue for value.

scenario: BB defends vs BTN open (BB calls). Flop 9♥8♥7♠, BB checks, BTN bets, BB raises. Hero (BTN) holds A♥A♣.
question: Categorize your hand strength and name the default plan.
options: Medium — pot control / proceed with caution
explanation: AA on 9-8-7 two-tone is an overpair — but on this VERY wet board, facing a check-raise, it's functionally MEDIUM-and-cautious, not an easy commit. The check-raising range (sets, two pair, made straights, big combo draws) is well ahead of or flipping against your overpair. You're IP as the aggressor; one pair (even aces) is not a stack-off here. Pot control / call once and reassess; don't get it all-in on 9-8-7 vs a check-raise. The board texture demotes even aces.
